author: @preztcs
text: Robert,  It is always a good idea to check the bed is flat with a straight edge before starting the leveling process. Quite Often (especially on the larger machines) the bed arrives slightly bent/bowed or dished and this can be a real headache for new people when trying to follow along with the leveling process. Also it is always a good idea to run through the leveling twice  to confirm the later point adjustments hasn't affected the first  points adjustments.

text: It's good practice when levelling to preheat your bed first. Also I find it better to level using stainless steel shim or a feeler gauge between bed and nozzle instead of paper. Another great video Rob many thanks.

author: @pikotech1
text:
Given the size of the last ugrinsky, which was about the size of Rob's head, looking at this one, you could maybe double the height and width. Might take a month or so to print, but if you've got it doing nothing else, it could be fun to see how well a larger version performs.

Hell, I reckon you could make some pretty impressive HAWT blades if you printed them individually.

Looking forward to seeing what you do with this mate. I'd love one, but can't justify the cost without a good plan to build something crazy unfortunately!

author: @monsterjoe76
text: Remember to absolutely square every angle of the printer (x, y & z) as acurate as you can before leveling the bed if you want acurate prints. If this is slightly out of angle it wil skew your prints, this isnt a big problem for most dayly prints but if your printing technical/mechanical parts that need to be acurate this will be important. For this reason i hate auto bed leveling for example, if one of the angles isnt exactly 90deg it wil skew the print. i use M5 locknuts to level my bed so the bed will always (99.9%) be level and if something goes wrong its probably one of the angles that got misaligned.  (btw i only used my own designed and build printers thus far, so cant judge about printers like this one and i dont know how and if you be able you aligns the angles without doin some macGuyer on it :)

author: @grendel1960a
text:
the only machine I know that is different is my flsun q5, this has 3 vertical supports and the nozzle is suspended between these, so the nozzle position changes with the height of the motors on the 3 legs. levelling is fully automatic, the machine just maps the bed using a probe that is temporarily mounted to the print head, once levelled the only part you need to do is set the z height with the piece of paper as you showed, if though you find the print is not sticking to the bed on the first pass you can quickly adjust the z height on the fly in 0.05mm increments, since the bed is firmly bolted down and not  moving the machine just adjusts the print using the mapped grid it has for the bed.
in this case the bed being level is not important as the machine plots its position relative to the bed using the 3 vertical motors, the machine has to compensate to get a level print anyway as its a factor of 3 coordinate positioning to get the printhead in the correct location.

author: @lv_woodturner3899
text: Interesting that the very large build plate has the two pivot points and six lateral adjustments.   This makes sense.   My Creality CR10S is "only" 300x300mm.    This has the common 4 points, one at each corner.   I would prefer if it also had one or two pivot points in the middle of the bed.
